ctx.name: Sam
ctx.notes: Sam is in week three and has started picking up real tickets, but he still hesitates before shipping and asks for reassurance late. I need to understand whether this is a normal confidence dip or a tooling and expectation gap we can fix quickly. I also want clarity on where onboarding handoffs are still fuzzy so he is not quietly compensating. This should stay practical and supportive: map what is unclear, unblock him, and line up the right support touchpoints for the next two weeks.

focusPoints.label: Shipping confidence and commitment timing.
focusPoints.reason: Sam hesitates before shipping and asks for reassurance late. Could be a normal confidence dip in week three, or a tooling/expectation gap about what “ready” means—worth clarifying what specifically triggers the delay.

focusPoints.label: Onboarding handoffs that still feel fuzzy.
focusPoints.reason: Notes point to unclear onboarding handoffs that Sam might be compensating for. One possibility is missing context at the boundaries; worth mapping which handoffs are unclear and what decision or artifact he’s waiting on.

focusPoints.label: Support touchpoints for the next two weeks.
focusPoints.reason: What he would want more of from you during onboarding is a concrete, lightweight support rhythm (who to ask, when to ask, and what “done enough to ship” looks like) so he doesn’t burn time seeking reassurance late.

focusPoints.label: Asking for help earlier, with the right context.
focusPoints.reason: How he communicates when he’s stuck is a fast onboarding lever for juniors—getting him to share what he tried, what looks uncertain, and what decision he needs can reduce late reassurance requests without lowering quality.

transcript.question: What’s been most on your mind at work this week, Sam?
transcript.answer: I can do the tasks, but I still second-guess whether my approach matches team expectations.

transcript.question: When you second-guess your approach, what feels hardest to read about team expectations?
transcript.answer: Local setup is better now, but I still lose time when the design and API details do not match.

transcript.question: When design and API details do not match, what is usually unclear first?
transcript.answer: I am not always sure who should approve what before I push.

transcript.question: What feels clear or unclear about what “done enough to ship” means here?
transcript.answer: I usually wait too long before asking because I want to solve things myself first.

transcript.question: When you hold off asking, what tells you it is time to pull someone in?
transcript.answer: When feedback is specific and fast, I move much quicker.

transcript.question: What kind of feedback helps most in those moments?
transcript.answer: I still feel fuzzy on where quality bars differ between quick fixes and core flows.

transcript.question: What tends to make you pause before shipping a ticket?
transcript.answer: Pairing once or twice a week would probably prevent me from getting stuck silently.

transcript.question: What kind of check-in from me would be most useful over the next two weeks?
transcript.answer: If we define what good looks like for my next two tickets, I will feel much steadier.

transcript.question: What’s the one thing you want to have changed by our next check-in?
